questions.q: Whose son was the warrior Barbarika?
questions.explanation: Barbarika was the son of Ghatotkacha, the giant born to Bhima and the rakshasi Hidimbi.

questions.q: Which Pandava was Barbarika's grandfather?
questions.explanation: As Ghatotkacha's son, Barbarika was the grandson of Bhima, the second Pandava.

questions.q: How many arrows did Barbarika claim he needed to finish the entire war?
questions.explanation: Barbarika carried three infallible arrows, which he said could end the war at Kurukshetra in moments.

questions.q: What vow governed Barbarika's part in any battle?
options: To never strike the first blow
To always fight for the weaker side
questions.explanation: Barbarika was bound by a vow to always support the weaker or losing side in any conflict.

questions.q: Who asked Barbarika for his head as alms before the war?
questions.explanation: Krishna, foreseeing the havoc Barbarika's vow would cause, asked for his head in charity before the first battle.

questions.q: In what guise was the request for Barbarika's head made?
questions.explanation: Krishna approached Barbarika disguised as a brahmin seeking alms, knowing the young warrior would refuse no gift.

questions.q: What did Barbarika's severed head do during the war?
options: It watched all eighteen days of battle
It slept until the final day
questions.explanation: Granted his last wish, Barbarika's head remained alive and witnessed the entire eighteen-day war.

questions.q: From where did Barbarika's head observe the fighting at Kurukshetra?
questions.explanation: Krishna placed the head atop a hill overlooking the battlefield so it could see every day of the war.

questions.q: Under which name is Barbarika widely worshipped today?
questions.explanation: Barbarika is venerated as Khatu Shyam, a beloved deity of the folk tradition centered in Rajasthan.

questions.q: Who was Barbarika's mother?
questions.explanation: Barbarika's mother is most commonly named Maurvi, though some retellings call her Ahilawati.

questions.q: When Krishna asked how quickly he could end the war, what did Barbarika reply?
options: Within one watch of the night
questions.explanation: Barbarika answered that with his three arrows he could finish the entire war in about a minute, astonishing Krishna.

questions.q: What test did Krishna set to prove the power of Barbarika's arrows?
options: Shooting out a lamp at midnight
Marking every leaf of a peepal tree
questions.explanation: Krishna challenged Barbarika to pierce all the leaves of a peepal tree, and a single arrow marked every one.

questions.q: After marking the tree's leaves, where did Barbarika's test arrow come to hover?
questions.explanation: The arrow circled and hovered over Krishna's foot, refusing to leave any marked leaf untouched.

questions.q: Why did the test arrow hover over Krishna's foot?
options: Krishna froze it with his will
Krishna had hidden a leaf beneath his foot
Its power was exhausted at last
questions.explanation: Krishna had secretly pressed one leaf under his foot, and the arrow waited to pierce it, proving nothing could escape the weapon.

questions.q: To whom had Barbarika given the promise that ruled his choice of sides in battle?
questions.explanation: Barbarika had promised his mother that he would always fight on behalf of whichever side was weaker.

questions.q: Which side did Barbarika intend to join first at Kurukshetra, and why?
options: The Kauravas, to repay a debt
Neither side, as a neutral judge
The Pandavas, because they were weaker
questions.explanation: Since the Pandavas had the smaller army, Barbarika's vow first placed him on their side.

questions.q: What outcome did Krishna foresee if Barbarika fought under his vow to aid the weaker side?
options: He would switch sides until he alone survived
He would be slain on the very first day
The war would simply never begin
The gods would descend to stop him
questions.explanation: Krishna explained that each side would become the weaker in turn, forcing Barbarika to oscillate until both armies were destroyed and only he remained.

questions.q: Why did Krishna say a head was required before the battle could begin?
options: To appease the war god Kartikeya
To fulfill an old curse upon the field
The field's consecration demanded the bravest kshatriya's sacrifice
To complete the count of eighteen armies
questions.explanation: In the popular telling, Krishna said the battlefield's worship required the sacrifice of the head of the bravest kshatriya, and Barbarika was the bravest.

questions.q: After the war, when the Pandavas debated who deserved credit for the victory, what did Barbarika's head declare?
options: That Arjuna's bow had won the war
That Krishna's discus alone had slain the armies
That Bhima's mace had decided everything
That fate, not warriors, had triumphed
questions.explanation: The head, having watched every day, said it saw only Krishna's discus slaying the warriors on both sides.

questions.q: What boon did Krishna grant Barbarika after accepting his head?
options: Worship in Kali Yuga by Krishna's own name, Shyam
questions.explanation: Pleased by the supreme gift, Krishna granted that Barbarika would be worshipped in the Kali Yuga by Krishna's own name, Shyam.

questions.q: In the popular telling, who bestowed the three infallible arrows on Barbarika?
questions.explanation: Barbarika earned the three arrows through austerities, most commonly said to be granted by Shiva, though some versions credit the goddesses.

questions.q: In which Indian state does the famous temple of Barbarika as Khatu Shyam stand?
questions.explanation: The Khatu Shyam temple stands at Khatu village in the Sikar district of Rajasthan.

questions.q: In which text does Barbarika's early story appear most fully?
questions.explanation: Barbarika's tale is told in the Kaumarika Khanda of the Skanda Purana, as he is absent from the critical text of Vyasa's epic.

questions.q: Barbarika's mother Maurvi is described as the daughter of which demon?
questions.explanation: Maurvi was the daughter of the demon Mura, the foe whom Krishna himself slew, an irony in Barbarika's lineage.

questions.q: In the lore of Barbarika's three arrows, what was the first arrow's task?
options: To mark everything meant for destruction
To shield its master from harm
To summon the other two arrows
questions.explanation: The first arrow marked all that was to be destroyed, the second marked what was to be spared, and the third destroyed everything marked.

questions.q: What did Brahma decree about Barbarika in his previous birth as a yaksha?
options: He would be reborn without memory
That Krishna would claim his head when Earth's burden was lifted
He would guard the northern mountains forever
questions.explanation: In the Skanda Purana the yaksha's boast drew Brahma's decree that Vishnu, as Krishna, would take his head at the time of relieving Earth's burden.

questions.q: Which epithet celebrates Barbarika specifically as the donor of his own head?
questions.explanation: Sheesh ke Daani literally means the giver of the head, honoring his ultimate act of charity to Krishna.

questions.q: Devotees hail Barbarika as Haare ka Sahara, a title meaning what?
questions.explanation: Haare ka Sahara means the refuge or support of the defeated, recalling his vow to stand with the losing side.

questions.q: In which Hindu month is the great annual fair of Khatu Shyam held?
questions.explanation: The famous Khatu Shyam fair is held each year in the month of Phalguna, drawing millions of pilgrims.

questions.q: Besides Krishna's discus, what did Barbarika's head say it saw devouring the battlefield?
options: Draupadi as the goddess drinking the blood of the slain
questions.explanation: In the popular telling, the head saw the discus slaying the warriors while Draupadi, as the fearsome goddess, drank the blood of the fallen.
